annotations.text: Did this patient have a community-acquired pneumonia?

ocystis carinii* is the most common single pathogen causing pneumonia in patients with AIDS, occurring in 80 percent of such patients during the course of their illness, I have to include it in the differential diagnosis of this patient's disease.

Several features of the case, however, are inconsistent with this diagnosis. Crackles and wheezing are rarely heard on examination of the chest in patients with pneumocystis pneumonia. Of more importance, the failure to recover the organisms by either induction of sputum or bronchoalveolar lavage and transbronchial biopsy, the progression of the interstitial infiltrates over a period of several months despite prophylaxis with aerosolized pentamidine, and finally the appearance of hilar lymphadenopathy are against a diagnosis of pneumocystis pneumonia.
I shall discard this diagnosis.
Although probably uncommon, pulmonary toxoplasmosis should be considered in this patient.

In the patient under discussion, the absence of involvement of other organs, such as the central nervous system, and the negative serologic test make the diagnosis of pulmonary toxoplasmosis improbable.
I shall now focus on the radiographic finding of pulmonary reticulonodular infiltrates with questionable hilar lymphadenopathy.

This tumor could indeed account for all the clinical and radiographic findings in the patient under discussion, including the wheezing, a possible episode of hemoptysis, and the episodes of high fever.
The absence of skin lesions and of the typical cherry-red, raised lesions in the tracheobronchial tree, however, would be unusual for a case of Kaposi's sarcoma.
Since a fairly well organized miliary pattern would be very unusual in both Kaposi's sarcoma and non-Hodgkin's lymphoma, I shall dismiss both diagnoses.
I come to the possibility of mycobacterial disease in this patient.
Because of these atypical features, tuberculosis in a patient with AIDS may easily be confused with other opportunistic infections.

However, the lack of positive smears on multiple specimens, the absence of obvious organ involvement outside the lungs, and the relatively slow evolution of the diffuse pulmonary infiltrates make a diagnosis of miliary tuberculosis less likely.
Because this patient remained relatively well and had no evidence of a gastrointestinal disturbance, I doubt very much that his pulmonary disease was caused by the  *M. avium–intracellulare* compl
I shall also exclude aspergillus as a potential pathogen, mainly because of the absence of appreciable predisposing factors, aside from HIV infection, in this patient.
Therefore, the absence of a positive test for cryptococcal antigen and the lack of recovery of cryptococcus from the lungs in this patient make the diagnosis of cryptococcosis unlikely.
I am finally led to consider infection with  *Histoplasma capsulatum* in this patie
Histoplasmosis has been reported in patients with AIDS from areas where this infection is endemic and from areas where it is not.
In one series of 18 cases of histoplasmosis seen in two New York hospitals, 16 patients were born in areas of endemic disease; 13 of them were from Puerto Rico.
In a large recently reported series of cases, fever and weight loss were the presenting manifestations in 96 percent of the patients.
The radiographs of the chest reveal reticulonodular, diffuse infiltrates in 41 percent of the cases and alveolar infiltrates in 17 percent of the cases.
The mild hyponatremia may have reflected a syndrome of inappropriate secretion of antidiuretic hormone or early adrenal insufficiency as a result of invasion of the adrenal gland by the organisms.

I favor progressive disseminated histoplasmosis as the principal diagnosis in this case because of the history of recent previous residence in an area where the infection is endemic and the relatively slow evolution of the disease, with minimal respiratory tract symptoms.

The diagnostic procedure performed on the 10th hospital day was probably an open-lung biopsy. Alternatively, a repeated bone marrow biopsy may have revealed the diagnosis, since this patient's disease was probably spread through the bloodstream.

If I am correct and this is a case of histoplasmosis, however, it is possible that the two episodes were related since the latency period between the onset of infection and the time of the diagnosis can be as long as six to eight months in this disease.

We believed that pneumocystis infection was possible because the serum LDH level was greater than 450 IU per liter and because the aerosolized pentamidine that he received would have decreased the yield of induction of sputum and bronchoscopic examination for that organism.

Histopathological examination revealed that the lung contained nodular aggregates of poorly formed granulomas immersed in a sea of lymphocytes. Some of the histiocytes in the infiltrate appeared to be undergoing lysis. Large histiocytes with abundant bubbly cytoplasm contained round or egg-shaped yeasts with distinct halos, which represented capsules.

The finding of histiocytes with cytoplasm packed with structures that stain poorly with hematoxylin in the lungs of patients with AIDS usually indicates  *M. avium–intracellulare* infection, but staining for acid-fast mycobacteria and for other organisms in this case was negati

In a series reported from New York City, 17 of 18 patients who had histoplasmosis and AIDS or risk factors for AIDS were Hispanic, whereas only a third of the overall population with AIDS at this hospital was Hispanic, and 13 of the 18 patients were born in Puerto Rico.
